html:
[408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 已去官亦死 - Concise statement indicating that the person who left office also died.
  • 而郡守中风不起 - Means 'the county magistrate suffered a stroke and did not recover.'
  • 相去才四十日 - Means 'there were only forty days between the two deaths.'
  • 吾一日退食 - Means 'on one day after I had finished my duties.'
  • 见四囚拜于下 - Means 'I saw four prisoners bowing before me.'
  • 某等枉死 - Means 'we were wrongfully killed.'
  • 诉于上帝得请矣 - Means 'we appealed to the Heavenly Emperor and obtained his approval.'
  • 欲逮公 - Means 'they want to arrest you.'
  • 所以知此冤而获吐者、黄司理力也 - Means 'the reason I know this injustice and can speak out is due to the efforts of Mr. Huang, the judicial official.'
  • 今七人已死 - Means 'now seven people have died.'
  • 足偿微命 - Means 'it is enough to atone for our minor lives.'
  • 乞勿追竟 - Means 'I beg that you do not pursue this further.'
  • 帝曰、使此人不书押 - Means 'the Heavenly Emperor said, 'If this person had not signed.'
  • 则汝四人不死 - Means 'then the four of you would not have died.'
  • 本于一押字 - Means 'it all stems from one signature.'
  • 原情定罪 - Means 'to determine guilt based on the circumstances.'
  • 此人其首也 - Means 'this person is the leader.'
  • 某等哭拜天廷 - Means 'we cried and bowed in the Heavenly Court.'
  • 凡四十九日 - Means 'for forty-nine days.'
  • 始许展三年 - Means 'they finally allowed a three-year delay.'
  • 即楦袴露膝 - Means 'immediately, the trousers were stuffed and the knees were exposed.'
  • 流血穿漏 - Means 'blood flowed through the holes.'
  • 拜不已 - Means 'continued to bow without stopping.'
  • 至于此 - Means 'until this point.'
  • 大限若满 - Means 'if the great deadline is reached.'
  • 当来此地相寻 - Means 'they will come to find you here.'
  • 又拜而去 - Means 'they bowed again and left.'
  • 吾适入门 - Means 'I just entered the door.'
  • 四囚已先在 - Means 'the four prisoners were already there.'
  • 云候伺已久 - Means 'they said they had been waiting for a long time.'
  • 恐过期 - Means 'fearing that the deadline had passed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 司理 - Refers to a judicial official responsible for legal matters.
  • 上帝 - Refers to the Heavenly Emperor, a figure in Chinese mythology who oversees justice and morality.
  • 中风 - Refers to a stroke, a medical condition that was often fatal in ancient times.
  • 天廷 - Refers to the Heavenly Court, where the Heavenly Emperor resides and judges cases.

Proper Nouns

  • 黄司理 - The name of the judicial official who helped uncover the injustice.
  • 上帝 - The Heavenly Emperor, a figure in Chinese mythology.

[409]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 且令亟取母妻与诀别 - Let him quickly fetch his mother and wife to say goodbye; expresses urgency and emotional weight.
  • 向曰、鬼安在 - Where are the ghosts?; prompts a response.
  • 黄指曰、皆拱立于此 - Yellow pointed and said, 'They are all standing here in a respectful manner'; describes a scene of ghostly presence.
  • 尔四人明灵若此 - The four of you are so clear and discerning; praises the ghosts' ability to understand.
  • 然厌厌无生意 - Yet he was listless and devoid of vitality; describes Huang's condition.
  • 又旬曰、告向曰、吾母已来 - After ten days, he told Xiang, 'My mother has come'; indicates a delayed response.
  • 幸为我办肩舆出迎 - Please prepare a sedan chair to meet her; expresses a need for assistance.
  • 果相遇于院门之外 - They indeed met outside the courtyard gate; describes an encounter.
  • 褰帘一揖而绝 - He lifted the curtain, bowed once, and then died; describes Huang's final act.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鬼 - Refers to spirits or ghosts; a common theme in Chinese folklore and religious beliefs.
  • 衣冠 - Refers to formal attire; symbolizes respect and propriety in rituals.
  • 肩舆 - Refers to a sedan chair; a mode of transport used by the elite in ancient China.
  • 乐平 - Refers to Yuleping; a place name, likely a historical region or city in China.
  • 侍郎 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy; indicates the social status of Yuanbo.

Proper Nouns

  • 向 - Refers to Xiang, a person in the story.
  • 郑 - Refers to Zheng, another person in the story.
  • 黄 - Refers to Huang, the main subject of the story.
  • 元伯 - Refers to Yuanbo, the son of Xiang and the narrator of the story.
  • 乐平 - Refers to Yuleping, a place name.

[410]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 設醮 - To hold a Daoist ritual, often for the purpose of seeking blessings or longevity.
  • 腰金施狨 - Describes the wearers of gold belts and silver-embroidered robes, indicating high status.
  • 張蓋者 - Refers to those who hold parasols or umbrellas, another sign of high rank.
  • 太師所敬也 - The respect of the Grand Tutor, referring to the highest-ranking official in the imperial court.
  • 齊先生適在此 - Master Qi happens to be here, indicating an unexpected presence.
  • 無一瓦蓋頭矣 - Not a single tile will cover their heads, a metaphor for the downfall of the Cai family.
  • 雖蹇驢亦無有矣 - Not even a lame donkey will be left, another metaphor for the downfall of the Cai family.
  • 哀哉 - An expression of sorrow or pity.
  • 三歲而蔡氏敗 - Three years later, the Cai family fell.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宣和五年 - The fifth year of the Xuanhe era (1123 AD).
  • 蔡魯公 - Cai Zhongxiang, a prominent official who had retired but still held significant influence.
  • 凝祥宮 - A temple or shrine where Daoist rituals were held.
  • 向元伯 - Yuanbo, the county magistrate in Kaifeng.
  • 曾徽言 - A government official who had a conflict with Cai Zhongxiang.
  • 齊先生 - Master Qi, a mysterious figure who appears unexpectedly and provides a prophetic warning.
  • 秋風落葉 - A metaphor for the decline and fall, often used to describe the downfall of powerful families.
  • 淄州人 - A place name, indicating Master Qi is from Zizhou.
  • 元伯說 - Indicates that the story is told by Yuanbo.

Proper Nouns

  • 宣和五年 - The fifth year of the Xuanhe era (1123 AD).
  • 蔡魯公 - Cai Zhongxiang, a retired official.
  • 凝祥宮 - A Daoist temple.
  • 向元伯 - Yuanbo, the county magistrate.
  • 曾徽言 - A government official.
  • 齊先生 - Master Qi, a mysterious figure.
  • 淄州 - A place name.
  • 元伯說 - Indicates the narrator.

[411]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 命道士设醮 - Commanding Taoist priests to perform a ritual for healing or blessing.
  • 倩所亲王生作青词 - Asking a close friend named Wang Sheng to write a green paper (a type of ritual text).
  • 连呼曰、请侍郎夫人来 - Continuously called out, asking for the wife of the Vice Minister.
  • 汝今归 - You now return.
  • 便与吾妻说 - Tell my wife.
  • 速营功果救我 - Quickly perform good deeds to save me.
  • 乃招路时中作黄箓醮 - Then she called Lu Shizhong to perform a yellow paper ritual.
  • 为谢罪请命 - To atone for the sins and pray for forgiveness.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宣和 - The era name of Emperor Huizong of Song, indicating the time period.
  • 户部 - The Ministry of Revenue, one of the six ministries in the Song Dynasty.
  • 侍郎 - A high-ranking official, equivalent to a minister.
  • 青州 - A prefecture in Shandong Province, China.
  • 道士设醮 - A Taoist ritual performed for healing or blessing.
  • 青词 - A type of ritual text written on green paper, used in Taoist ceremonies.
  • 黄箓醮 - A Taoist ritual for atonement and forgiveness.
  • 梁山泺 - A region known for its bandits during the Song Dynasty.
  • 郓州 - A prefecture in Shandong Province, where Patsui Hou was the military governor.

Proper Nouns

  • 蔡居厚 - The name of the historical figure.
  • 青州 - The name of the prefecture.
  • 金陵 - The name of the city, modern-day Nanjing.
  • 王生 - The name of the close friend.
  • 路时中 - The name of the Taoist priest.
  • 梁山泺 - The name of the region.
  • 郓州 - The name of the prefecture.

[412]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 杳氏村祖 - Compound noun meaning 'the ancestral spirits of the village of Yao.'
  • 憧憧往来 - Vivid expression describing a bustling, busy scene where people are moving back and forth.
  • 兀然如土木 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'motionless like wood or stone,' emphasizing the immobility of the elderly couple.
  • 伸舌舐之 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'extend their tongues to lick,' indicating a ritualistic or respectful gesture.
  • 皮皆傅骨 - Phrase meaning 'skin tightly adhering to the bones,' suggesting the elderly couple's advanced age and frailty.
  • 不知几百岁 - Estimate meaning 'do not know how many hundreds of years,' indicating the perceived longevity of the elderly couple.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 赣州光孝首坐僧普瑞 - Refers to a monk named Pu Rui, who was the chief monk at Guangxiao Temple in Ganzhou. Provides historical and geographical context.
  • 江州通判船 - Refers to a boat belonging to a local government official (通判) in Jiangzhou. Indicates the setting and the social status of the official.
  • 池州 - A city in Anhui Province, providing a specific location for the events described.
  • 供佛处 - A place where people worship Buddha, indicating the religious context of the scene.
  • 翁媪二人 - Refers to an elderly couple, typically used to describe married elderly individuals.
  • 以衣衾拥下体 - Describes the elderly couple wearing a type of clothing (衣衾) that covers their lower bodies, indicating their modesty and simplicity.
  • 衣衾 - A type of clothing, possibly a traditional robe or garment.
  • 香灯酌酒以供 - Describes the offerings made to the elderly couple, including incense, lamps, and wine, indicating a ritualistic or respectful gesture.
  • 撮作小包 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'make small packets of the clothing,' indicating a ritualistic preparation.
  • 蘸酒置二老口 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'dip the packets in wine and place them in the mouths of the two elders,' indicating a ritualistic or respectful gesture.
  • 引手摸其胸乳 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'extend their hands to touch the chests of the two elders,' indicating a ritualistic or respectful gesture.
  • 傅骨 - Phrase meaning 'adhering to the bones,' suggesting the elderly couple's advanced age and frailty.
  • 几百岁 - Estimate meaning 'hundreds of years,' indicating the perceived longevity of the elderly couple.
  • 杳氏村 - Refers to the village of Yao, providing a specific location for the events described.
  • 此二老为村祖云 - Concluding statement meaning 'these two elders are the village ancestors,' providing the final identification of the elderly couple.

Proper Nouns

  • 赣州 - A city in Jiangxi Province.
  • 光孝 - The name of the temple, Guangxiao Temple.
  • 首坐僧普瑞 - The name of the monk, Pu Rui.
  • 江州 - A city in Jiangxi Province.
  • 通判 - The title of the local government official.
  • 池州 - A city in Anhui Province.
  • 杳氏村 - The name of the village, Yao Village.
  • 翁媪 - The elderly couple.
  • 衣衾 - A type of clothing.
  • 香灯 - Incense and lamps.
  • 酌酒 - Wine.
  • 钱烛 - Money and candles.
  • 茶酒 - Tea and wine.
  • 胸乳 - Chest and breasts.
  • 傅骨 - Adhering to the bones.
  • 几百岁 - Hundreds of years.
  • 村祖 - Village ancestors.

[413]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 噀手嘻笑曰 - Vivid description of spitting out saliva while laughing, indicating a display of arrogance or contempt.
  • 甘心焉 - Means 'to take pleasure in doing something,' often with a negative connotation.
  • 蠒痛 - Refers to a severe, throbbing pain, possibly due to a wound or injury.
  • 告假归 - Means 'to take leave and return home,' indicating a temporary departure from duty.
  • 逾月创始愈 - Means 'after more than a month, the initial pain subsided,' indicating a gradual recovery.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 建康 - The capital of the Eastern Jin and Southern Dynasties, located in present-day Nanjing, China.
  • 伍伯 - A military or administrative title, often associated with authority and power.
  • 朱衣吏 - A low-ranking official or servant, often depicted as subservient to higher authorities.
  • 紫姑神 - Refers to a deity or spiritual entity, often associated with divination or supernatural events.
  • 唐进士刘叉 - Refers to Liu Cha, a Tang Dynasty poet and scholar, known for his eccentric behavior and literary works.

Proper Nouns

  • 建康 - The capital of the Eastern Jin and Southern Dynasties, located in present-day Nanjing, China.
  • 伍伯 - A military or administrative title, often associated with authority and power.
  • 朱衣吏 - A low-ranking official or servant, often depicted as subservient to higher authorities.
  • 紫姑神 - Refers to a deity or spiritual entity, often associated with divination or supernatural events.
  • 唐进士刘叉 - Refers to Liu Cha, a Tang Dynasty poet and scholar, known for his eccentric behavior and literary works.

[414]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 聊为一启 - Formal expression meaning 'I will briefly write to you' or 'I will briefly address you.'
  • 思史之谬词 - Refers to erroneous statements or misleading accounts in historical records.
  • 昔之异行 - Means 'past unusual actions' or 'past extraordinary deeds.'
  • 令余忸然感叹 - Expresses the author's feeling of shame and regret.
  • 余终于终南 - Means 'I ultimately passed away in Zhongnan.'
  • 门人葬于山之阳 - Means 'my disciples buried me on the sunny side of the mountain.'
  • 清溪之侧 - Refers to 'near a clear stream.'
  • 至今坟犹在 - Means 'the tomb still exists today.'
  • 但人不知为余墓也 - Means 'but people do not know it is my tomb.'
  • 以余无勋庸于国 - Means 'because I had no military achievements for the country.'
  • 故史氏听小人之言 - Means 'thus the historians listened to the words of the wicked.'
  • 书不知所终 - Means 'the records do not know where they ended.'
  • 设如子仪光弼辈 - Refers to 'suppose figures like Guo Ziyi and Li Guangbi.'
  • 后世皆知其大功 - Means 'later generations all know their great achievements.'
  • 然当时史词褒饰甚多 - Means 'but at the time, the historians' words were greatly praised and embellished.'
  • 盖世之情如斯也 - Means 'the world's sentiment is like this.'
  • 呜呼 - An exclamation expressing sorrow or regret.
  • 尽信史则不如无史 - Means 'to completely trust history is worse than having no history.'
  • 彼若不能摭实 - Means 'if they cannot get to the truth.'
  • 但务华以媚天子 - Means 'but only strive for elegance to flatter the emperor.'
  • 自可询有知而书之 - Means 'they can certainly consult those who know and write it down.'
  • 何必纵缪言诬介义之士于有过之地哉 - Means 'why should they allow false words to slander the righteous and virtuous in inappropriate places?'
  • 使余当时闻之 - Means 'if I had heard of it at the time.'
  • 必令此佞夫首足异处 - Means 'I would certainly make this flatterer's head and feet be in different places.'
  • 余既为仙不复竞 - Means 'since I have become a deity, I no longer compete.'
  • 姑隐之 - Means 'I will keep it hidden for now.'
  • 后世哲者共为我鉴诸 - Means 'may the wise of later generations take this as a lesson.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 史氏 - Refers to historians or historical records.
  • 小人 - Refers to wicked or lowly individuals.
  • 子仪光弼辈 - Refers to Guo Ziyi and Li Guangbi, famous generals of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 终南 - Refers to Zhongnan Mountain, a famous mountain in Shaanxi Province.
  • 清溪 - Refers to a clear stream, likely a specific location near Zhongnan Mountain.
  • 勋庸 - Refers to military achievements and contributions.
  • 盖世之情 - Refers to the general sentiment or public opinion of the time.
  • 华 - Refers to elegance or refinement.
  • 天子 - Refers to the emperor.
  • 佞夫 - Refers to flatterers or sycophants.
  • 介义之士 - Refers to righteous and virtuous individuals.
  • 仙 - Refers to deities or immortals.

Proper Nouns

  • 子仪 - Guo Ziyi, a famous general of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 光弼 - Li Guangbi, another famous general of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 终南 - Zhongnan Mountain, a famous mountain in Shaanxi Province.
  • 清溪 - A clear stream, likely a specific location near Zhongnan Mountain.
  • 天子 - The emperor.
  • 仙 - Deities or immortals.

[415]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 猪足符 - Literal phrase meaning 'pig's hoof talisman'; seems to be a specific term or object mentioned in the text.
  • 细民 - Refers to ordinary people or commoners.
  • 举债 - Means 'to borrow money.'
  • 庖婢 - Refers to a kitchen maid or female servant.
  • 亟出告 - Means 'hurriedly came out to report.'
  • 屠杌 - Refers to a butcher or a place where butchering takes place.
  • 责谯之 - Means 'to scold or reprimand.'
  • 元直 - Term that is unclear in context; might refer to the original or intact item.
  • 五事皆郏次南说 - Ambiguous phrase requiring further research to understand its meaning.
  • 庙神止奏章 - Refers to a temple deity stopping a petition or offering.
  • 岁满必迁 - Means 'at the end of their term, they must move on.'
  • 主此地若干岁 - Means 'has been in charge of this place for several years.'
  • 闻其家将奏章 - Means 'heard that the family was going to offer a petition.'
  • 丐君一言 - Means 'plead with you for a word.'
  • 恳请至再三 - Means 'pleaded repeatedly.'
  • 许诺 - Means 'promised.'
  • 问其所止 - Means 'asked where it was located.'
  • 皮场庙 - Refers to a temple dedicated to a deity associated with leather or skin.
  • 如神言告其邻 - Means 'as the deity said, told the neighbor.'
  • 止不奏 - Means 'stopped the offering.'
  • 即日愈 - Means 'improved on the same day.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 聂景言、居衡阳 - Refers to a person named Nie Jingyan living in Hengyang, a city in Hunan Province.
  • 细民 - Commoners or ordinary people.
  • 猪蹄 - A common food item in Chinese cuisine.
  • 庖婢 - Kitchen maid, a common role in traditional Chinese households.
  • 屠杌 - Butcher, a common occupation.
  • 庙神 - Temple deity, a common figure in Chinese religious practices.
  • 岁满必迁 - Concept related to the rotation of deities in charge of different areas.
  • 皮场庙 - Specific type of temple, possibly dedicated to a deity associated with leather or skin.
  • 五事皆郏次南说 - Ambiguous phrase requiring further research to understand its meaning.

Proper Nouns

  • 聂景言 - A person's name.
  • 衡阳 - A city in Hunan Province.
  • 段元肃 - A person's name.
  • 京师 - The capital city, likely referring to Beijing.
  • 五事皆郏次南说 - Ambiguous phrase requiring further research to understand its meaning.
  • 皮场庙 - A specific type of temple.

[417]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 心恋恋不已 - Deeply attached, constantly missing.
  • 天心王法 - A specific Taoist ritual or practice used to exorcise or dispel supernatural influences.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宗室 - Refers to the royal or noble family, indicating the social status of the protagonist.
  • 道人 - Refers to a Taoist monk, indicating a figure of spiritual authority.
  • 鬼 - Refers to spirits or ghosts, which are common themes in Chinese folklore and literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 赵子举 - The name of the protagonist.
  • 升之 - The courtesy name of the protagonist.
  • 道人 - The Taoist monk who appears in the story.
  • 天心王法 - A specific Taoist ritual or practice.

[418]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 繪六甲六丁像 - Literally 'paint images of the Six Jia and Six Ding,' referring to the Six Jia and Six Ding, which are terms from ancient Chinese astrology and divination. The Six Jia (甲、乙、丙、丁、戊、己) and Six Ding (子、丑、寅、卯、辰、巳) are associated with celestial deities and are often invoked in rituals.
  • 齋戒奉事唯谨 - Literally 'fast and worship with utmost caution,' indicating a strict and reverent approach to religious rituals.
  • 妻犹如故态 - Literally 'the wife remains in her old state,' suggesting that the wife continues to behave as she did before.
  • 颇亦不乐 - Literally 'she is also not happy,' indicating a change in the wife's mood.
  • 时时长吁 - Literally 'often sighs deeply,' suggesting a sense of distress or dissatisfaction.
  • 如不得志者 - Literally 'as if she were not achieving her goals,' indicating a sense of unfulfilled desires.
  • 涕泣辞诀 - Literally 'cry and bid farewell,' indicating a tearful goodbye.
  • 久留恐坏君法 - Literally 'fearing that staying too long would corrupt your law,' suggesting a concern about the negative impact of prolonged presence.
  • 遂绝不至 - Literally 'and then never came again,' indicating a permanent departure.
  • 子举从此奉法愈力 - Literally 'from then on, Zi Ju observes the law with even greater diligence,' indicating a renewed commitment to religious practices.
  • 为人治病辄验 - Literally 'treats people's illnesses and always succeeds,' indicating a high success rate in medical treatments.
  • 建炎二年 - The year 1128 in the Jianyan era of the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 予妻族张氏、避地自京师南下 - Literally 'my wife's family, the Zhang clan, fled south from the capital,' indicating a migration due to war or other disturbances.
  • 寓居扬州龙兴寺 - Literally 'reside temporarily in Longxing Temple in Yangzhou,' indicating a place of temporary residence.
  • 先是有祖姑嫁赵氏 - Literally 'previously, an aunt married into the Zhao family,' indicating a historical context.
  • 夫为绛州守 - Literally 'her husband was the governor of Jiangzhou,' indicating a high-ranking official position.
  • 值虏骑围城 - Literally 'when the barbarian cavalry surrounded the city,' indicating a military siege.
  • 姑陨于炮下 - Literally 'the aunt died under the bombardment,' indicating a tragic death.
  • 又有八叔者、为贼所得 - Literally 'there was also an uncle who was captured by the bandits,' indicating another tragic event.
  • 脔食之 - Literally 'cut into pieces and eaten,' indicating a brutal and inhumane act.
  • 是岁妻祖母田氏病 - Literally 'that year, my wife's grandmother, Lady Tian, fell ill,' indicating a specific event.
  • 仿佛见此两人在窗外 - Literally 'vaguely saw these two people outside the window,' indicating a ghostly or supernatural appearance.
  • 子举适同居寺中 - Literally 'Zi Ju happened to be staying in the temple at the same time,' indicating a coincidental presence.
  • 外舅以事告之 - Literally 'my father-in-law informed him about the matter,' indicating a communication of information.
  • 子举焚香祷请 - Literally 'Zi Ju burned incense and prayed,' indicating a religious ritual.
  • 久而言曰 - Literally 'after a long time, he said,' indicating a delayed response.
  • 皆以非命死 - Literally 'both died by unnatural means,' indicating a violent or untimely death.
  • 然是公家戚属 - Literally 'but they are part of your family,' indicating a familial connection.
  • 不宜加罪 - Literally 'should not be punished,' indicating a moral consideration.
  • 当以酒币善遣之 - Literally 'should be sent away with wine and gifts,' indicating a proper way to handle the situation.
  • 如其言 - Literally 'as he said,' indicating compliance with the advice.
  • 病亦寻愈 - Literally 'the illness also soon recovered,' indicating a positive outcome.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 六甲六丁 - Refers to the Six Jia and Six Ding, which are terms from ancient Chinese astrology and divination. They are often associated with celestial deities and are invoked in rituals for protection and guidance.
  • 建炎 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Song, the first emperor of the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 绛州 - An ancient state in Shanxi Province, known for its strategic importance.
  • 太原 - The capital of Shanxi Province, a major city in northern China.
  • 虏骑 - Refers to the Jurchen (女真) cavalry, the invaders who established the Jin Dynasty and caused significant disruption in northern China.
  • 贼 - Refers to bandits or rebels, often used in the context of internal strife or rebellion.
  • 龙兴寺 - A Buddhist temple in Yangzhou, known for its historical significance and religious practices.

Proper Nouns

  • 六甲六丁 - The Six Jia and Six Ding, terms from ancient Chinese astrology and divination.
  • 建炎 - The reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Song.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '何丞相在太学' introduces He Wenzheng and his position in the Imperial Academy.
  • The clause '与同舍生黄君、诣日者孙黯问命' describes the action of visiting the fortune-teller, with the subject being He Wenzheng and his companion.
  • The phrase '黯袒衣踞坐' describes the fortune-teller's posture, which is informal and casual.
  • The clause '命极贵，不惟魁天下，且位极人臣' provides the fortune-teller's prediction, which is both grand and specific.
  • The phrase '二人相视笑' describes the reaction of He Wenzheng and his companion to the fortune-teller's prediction.
  • The phrase '何相侮邪' is a rhetorical question expressing surprise or disbelief.
  • The phrase '黯愠曰' marks the fortune-teller's response, which is angry.
  • The clause '黯老矣，粗有生计，今谄一秀才，其获几何' explains the fortune-teller's motivation and the limited benefit he expects from flattering a scholar.
  • The phrase '柰何命实中格' acknowledges the accuracy of the fortune-teller's prediction.
  • The phrase '然则何时作状元' asks about the specific time when He Wenzheng will become the top scholar.
  • The phrase '何年为相' asks about the specific year when He Wenzheng will become a high-ranking official.
  • The phrase '不出一纪' provides a time frame for He Wenzheng's rise to power.
  • The phrase '但有一事绝异' introduces an unusual aspect of the prediction.
  • The clause '君拜相后，当死于异国' describes the unusual aspect of the prediction, which is that He Wenzheng will die in a foreign land after becoming a high-ranking official.
  • The clause '寻常奉使绝域者，不过侍从官，何由有宰相入国者' explains why the prediction is unusual, as it is uncommon for high-ranking officials to be sent to distant lands.
  • The phrase '此为不可晓耳' concludes the fortune-teller's explanation, emphasizing the unusual nature of the prediction.
  • The clause '初丞相自仙井来，时过桐柏，于庙中上书乞梦' provides background information about He Wenzheng's actions and motivations.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 两人悔前日空反 - The two regret returning empty-handed the previous day, '悔前日空反' indicating regret for not finding what they sought.
  • 乃相约重寻之 - Decides to search again together, '乃相约重寻之' meaning 'decides to search again together.'
  • 历三日、与曩所记无异 - After three days, nothing differs from what was previously marked, '历三日' meaning 'after three days.'
  • 则茅屋已焚 - The thatched house has been burned down, '则茅屋已焚' indicating the destruction of the house.
  • 但斫木皮尺余 - Only cuts the bark off the wood for a few inches, '斫木皮尺余' meaning 'cuts the bark off the wood for a few inches.'
  • 题诗其间曰 - Writes a poem on it, '题诗其间' meaning 'writes a poem on it.'

  • 怅然而归 - Returns with a sense of disappointment or melancholy, '怅然而归' indicating a feeling of loss or regret.
  • 后无有能去者 - Afterward, no one can go there, '后无有能去者' indicating that the place is no longer accessible.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 宫人不知辇至 - Literal meaning: 'The palace attendants did not know the sedan chair had arrived.' Sets the scene of the emperor's unexpected arrival.
  • 或晚起 - Literal meaning: 'Or they might wake up late.' Suggests a casual or relaxed atmosphere among the palace attendants.
  • 才画一眉 - Literal meaning: 'Having just drawn one eyebrow.' Implies a hurried or incomplete state of preparation.
  • 趋出迎谒 - Literal meaning: 'Rushing out to greet and pay homage.' Describes the attendants' quick and respectful response to the emperor's arrival.
  • 帝顾之笑 - Literal meaning: 'The emperor looked at them and smiled.' Indicates the emperor's amusement or affection.
  • 命诸侍郎赋诗 - Literal meaning: 'Ordered the officials to compose poetry.' Shows the emperor's appreciation for literary talent.
  • 先生卒章云 - Literal meaning: 'The scholar's concluding lines said.' Introduces the scholar's poetic response.
  • 晓妆不觉星舆至 - Literal meaning: 'In the morning makeup, unaware the imperial carriage had arrived.' Reflects the scholar's preoccupation with her appearance.
  • 只画人间一壁眉 - Literal meaning: 'Only drawing one eyebrow on the wall of the human world.' Suggests a sense of incompleteness or irony.
  • 帝吟讽激赏 - Literal meaning: 'The emperor recited and praised it enthusiastically.' Indicates the emperor's high regard for the poem.
  • 为众所嫉 - Literal meaning: 'Being envied by many.' Suggests that the scholar's success and favor with the emperor caused jealousy.
  • 下迁群玉外监 - Literal meaning: 'Was demoted to the outer supervisor of the Jade Pavilion.' Indicates a change in the scholar's position.
  • 既陛辞 - Literal meaning: 'After bidding farewell at the palace steps.' Marks the moment of the scholar's departure.
  • 群玉殿乃吾图书之府 - Literal meaning: 'The Jade Pavilion is my repository of books.' Shows the emperor's regard for the scholar's literary abilities.
  • 非卿文学出伦 - Literal meaning: 'Not because of your extraordinary literary talent.' A rhetorical device to emphasize the scholar's worth.
  • 是后宴见稍疏 - Literal meaning: 'After that, the banquets and audiences became less frequent.' Indicates a cooling of the relationship between the scholar and the emperor.
  • 一日、帝与诸仙游瑶圃 - Literal meaning: 'One day, the emperor and the immortals toured the Jade Garden.' Sets the scene of a fantastical and divine encounter.
  • 思先生之材 - Literal meaning: 'Thinking of the scholar's talent.' Shows the emperor's continued appreciation for the scholar.
  • 遣使来召 - Literal meaning: 'Sent a messenger to summon.' Indicates the emperor's decision to recall the scholar.
  • 先生辞以疾 - Literal meaning: 'The scholar excused himself with illness.' Suggests the scholar's reluctance or inability to return.
  • 独与侍女宋道华泛舟池上 - Literal meaning: 'Alone with the maidservant Song Dao Hua, they boated on the pond.' Describes a private and intimate moment.
  • 执手眷眷 - Literal meaning: 'Holding hands affectionately.' Indicates a close and tender relationship.
  • 有人间夫妇之想 - Literal meaning: 'Having thoughts of human husband and wife.' Suggests a romantic or marital desire.
  • 为使者所劾 - Literal meaning: 'Was accused by the messenger.' Indicates the scholar's downfall due to the accusation.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '南临番江' can be read as either 'south-facing Pan River' or 'tower facing south, near the Pan River.'
  • The phrase '同登楼凭栏立' can be read as 'together ascend the tower and lean against the railing' or 'together ascend the tower, leaning against the railing.'
  • The phrase '有妇人不知所从来' can be read as 'a woman appeared without knowing her origin' or 'a woman appeared, not knowing where she came from.'
  • The phrase '已无所睹' can be read as 'she had already disappeared' or 'they no longer saw her.'
  • The phrase '急下楼' can be read as 'quickly descended the tower' or 'quickly went down the tower.'
  • The phrase '后不敢复往' can be read as 'later dared not go again' or 'later did not dare to go again.'
  • The phrase '郭□已说' can be read as 'Guo has already spoken about this' or 'someone named Guo has already mentioned this.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吾雖至愚 - Humble self-deprecating statement meaning 'Although I am extremely foolish.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秘术 - Refers to secret or esoteric techniques, often associated with Daoist practices.
  • 摩以岁月 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to refine over time,' often used in Daoist contexts to describe the gradual attainment of spiritual enlightenment.
  • 茅 - Refers to the herb *máo*, which is often associated with Daoist alchemy and spiritual practices.
  • 金钗 - A golden hairpin, often used as a symbol of wealth and status in Chinese culture.
  • 幻术 - Refers to illusions or magic tricks, often associated with Daoist or Buddhist practices.
  • 先生 - A respectful title for a teacher or master, often used in Daoist and Confucian contexts.
  • 上帝 - Refers to the Heavenly Deity, often associated with the Taoist concept of the Dao or the supreme being.
  • 云汉间 - Refers to the heavens, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe a lofty or divine realm.
  • 委曲 - Means 'indirectly' or 'circumstantially,' often used in the context of divine or spiritual intervention.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 吾虽至愚，然闻得道者，非积阴功至行，不可侥冀 - This sentence is a concessive statement, acknowledging the speaker's perceived foolishness but asserting that the principles of Daoism still apply.
  • 君假此辅道，摩以岁月，傥遂如愿 - This sequence is a conditional statement, suggesting that if the listener follows the speaker's advice, they may achieve their goal.
  • 戏拔茅一茎嘘之，则成金钗 - This is a metaphorical statement, using the transformation of a blade of grass into a golden hairpin to illustrate the power of the speaker's method.
  • 君未见其人，正遇之何以识 - This is a rhetorical question, emphasizing the difficulty of recognizing the master without prior knowledge.
  • 然则君三拜我，我能令君见 - This is a conditional statement, suggesting that if the listener performs a certain action, the speaker can fulfill their wish.
  • 视之无复樵容，俨然与所图无少异 - This is a descriptive statement, comparing the appearance of the master to a drawing.
  • 汝哀号数年，声彻云汉间，上帝亦深怜汝志 - This is a narrative statement, describing the listener's devotion and the divine response.
  • 故令吾委曲唤汝，汝从我去 - This is a causal statement, explaining the reason for the speaker's intervention and the subsequent action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sequence '溪流汹涌崩腾，木石皆振' describes the force of the stream and its effects on the environment.
  • The sequence '弱竹袅袅，不可着脚' describes the instability of the bamboo.
  • The sequence '适逢樵者出，乃前再拜' introduces the woodcutter and the monk's respectful greeting.
  • The sequence '樵者矍然退避曰，山中野人，采薪以供家，安敢当此' describes the woodcutter's startled reaction and his explanation of his role.
  • The sequence '具以所欲拱白之' describes the monk's formal request.
  • The sequence '樵始秘不言，既而曰，闻诸庵中女' describes the woodcutter's initial secrecy and subsequent revelation.
  • The sequence '多口老婆，妄泄吾事' describes the woodcutter's anger and the reason for his secrecy.
  • The sequence '令道人闭目，挽其衣以行' describes the woodcutter's command and action.
  • The sequence '觉如腾虚空，云龙出没' describes the monk's sensation of flying through the void.
  • The sequence '洞两耳间，既履地' describes the passage and the return to solid ground.
  • The sequence '宫殿崔嵬，金铺玉户' describes the grand and luxurious palaces.
  • The sequence '顾左右曰，安得有凡气' describes the blue-capped official's surprise.
  • The sequence '趋出稽首，碧冠叱曰' describes the monk's respectful greeting and the official's rebuke.
  • The sequence '谁引汝来，以樵者告' describes the official's question and the woodcutter's answer.
  • The sequence '袒其背，以铁拄杖鞭之三百六十' describes the punishment.
  • The sequence '血肉分离，骨破髓出' describes the severity of the punishment.
